data.title: Stocks Go Orbital On Trump's Tariff Timeout
data.description:
Wall Street jettisoned gravity today as President Trump called a 90-day armistice in his on-again, off-again trade war. A record 30 billion shares took flight in the best showing since 2008 and the Nasdaq’s second-best performance on record.

Level Change 4/9/25 (%)

+7.9 Dow

+12.2 Nasdaq

+12.1 Nasdaq 100

+9.5 S&P 500

+9.3 S&P 400

+8.8 S&P 600

The day began in a blue funk, courtesy of Beijing’s decision to lift tariffs on US goods to 84% from 34%. Talking heads groaned that a structural rebalance of global trade is underway, and not the sort likely to earn applause on either side of the Pacific.

Reports surfaced of companies raising prices to stay afloat amid the tariff squall. Analysts dutifully trimmed their S&P 500 targets. With Q1 earnings looming, Wall Street’s latest parlor game is guessing whether companies will even bother offering guidance. On a floor this slick, would you?

But the big overhang was the bond market, where analysts whispered about a “disorderly forced liquidation” - someone’s spreadsheet went up in smoke. Yields spiked as the so-called basis trade fell apart like a rental tux at closing time.

This arbitrage strategy involves shorting Treasury futures while buying the actual bonds (or the reverse), aiming to pocket the sliver of difference - the basis - when prices converge.

But when the spread moves the wrong way, traders don’t stroll to the exits; they stampede. Bonds get dumped, futures get bought back, and the whole trade flips from tidy to terrifying in a Trumpian minute. That’s what looked to be unfolding in the three days leading up to this morning.

On Monday and Tuesday, the 30-year Treasury notched its biggest yield surge since the pandemic began, a move that sent the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT +0.6%) tumbling 4.9% over the two days, a full-on faceplant by long-bond standards. Over on LinkedIn, Jim Bianco of Bianco Research dubbed it a “tell your grandchildren I was there” kind of day in the bond market.

Rumor has it that it wasn’t stocks, but imploding bonds, that forced Trump to tap the brakes on tariffs. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent denies this, of course. Reporters noted yields rising faster than a bad smell in a small elevator. They asked whether fears about a liquidity crisis and Treasuries losing their safe-haven status spurred Trump’s tactical timeout.

Officially, no. Unofficially, let’s just say the timing was exquisite.

“This was driven by the president’s strategy,” Bessent swore. “He and I had a long talk on Sunday, and this was his strategy all along.” On Fox Business, he offered further reassurance that these “deleveraging convulsions” happen now and then, no biggie.

Well, it was a biggie to potential home buyers, whose stock portfolios tanked as interest rates rose, a most unwelcome housewarming gift from the bond market.

But never mind the home buyers, the big takeaway is that the Trump Put exists after all, just lower than long imagined. Stocks can fend for themselves, but when the bond market raises its voice, Team Trump drops the act - as Bessent… denies. The plan all along? Sure thing, Scott. Plan?

Tonight, crack or pop something in celebration, just don’t get carried away. Universal 10% tariffs are still tugging at profits, inflation is all the Fed can talk about, US-China tension shows no signs of retreat (Trump just hiked China tariffs to 125%), and the chaos crew still holds the remote.

Volatility hasn’t left the building. It may have just stepped out for air.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Voya Global Advantage And Premium Opportunity Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Fund combines an actively-managed quantitative equity investment strategy with a call writing option strategy, seeking to create a diversified portfolio with enhanced total return potential and strong downside capture over a full market cycle.

Key takeaways

• During the quarter, the Fund benefited from the strong performance of its equity sleeve, particularly the operations pillar.

• U.S. stocks remain attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence.

• Outside of the U.S., pockets of weakness in China and the eurozone have become more apparent.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund provided a total return of 0.36% on a net asset value (NAV) basis and a total return of 0.31% on a market price -4.20%.

All five pillars within the model were positive, with the operations pillar performing the strongest. The fund's lower beta positioning was the second-largest contributor. Dividend yield and size were the largest detractors from performance.

On the regional level, portfolio holdings in the U.S. contributed and holdings in Japan detracted.

On the sector level, stock selection in the utilities, health care and industrials sectors contributed the most to results. Among the key individual stock contributors were the non-benchmark position in Unum Group (UNM), the overweight in Kinder Morgan Inc. (KMI) and a non-benchmark position in DT Midstream, Inc (DTM).

By contrast, stock selection in the communication services, financials and consumer discretionary sectors detracted. Among the key detractors were not owning JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), and Bank of America Corp. (BAC) as well as an overweight in Elevance Health, Inc (ELV).

Option portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a positive impact on returns. The strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implements this strategy by typically writing call options on regional indices, the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to track the reference Index of the Fund closely. The strike prices of the options written were typically out of the money or near the money with expiration dates around six weeks at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to continue its gradual easing.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China.

The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of Fund investments, as of 12/31/24: in Unum Group 0.53%, Kinder Morgan Inc. 0.70%, DT Midstream, Inc. 0.60%, JPMorgan Chase & Co. 0.00%, Bank of America Corp. 0.00% and Elevance Health, Inc. 0.74%; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change daily.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JPMorgan Earnings Preview: Tough Compares Meet Harder Capital Markets
data.description:
Now the world’s premier banking institution, with the banking world’s premier CEO, JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM) will report their Q1 ’25 financial results before the opening bell on Friday, April 11th, 2025.

Consensus estimates for the bank giant are expecting $4.61 in earnings per share (EPS) on $44.1 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth in EPS of exactly 0% (flat y-o-y) on -1% revenue growth.

The problem with JPM is history: 2023 and 2024 EPS growth was +23% and +15% respectively, while revenue growth in ’23 and ’24 grew 23% and +12 respectively. The bank – as good as it is – was punching above its weight class the last two years, supported by good capital markets a robust US economy.

It’s likely that 2025 will be a middling year for the bank giant. A lot of sell-side firms thought the IPO and M&A business, which was thought to have a lot of pent-up deals waiting to get done, seemed to fizzle in Q1 ’25. High expectations wound up meeting middling reality.

JPM’s CIB, which is the Corporate and Investment Bank segment, which represented 40% in net revenue and 47% of segment operating profits in Q4 ’24, might see their results return to the 8-quarter average of 33% and 34% respectively in 2025.

Valuation

With the drop in JPM’s stock price from $280 late in ’24 to $235 today (after the big rally), the P/E has compressed a little bit on JPM, as the EPS estimates haven’t changed much with the market turmoil.

At $235 per share, JPM is trading at 13x and 12x 2025 and 2026 EPS of $18.22 and $19.46, for expected y-o-y growth of 0% and 7% respectively.

JPM was always the priciest bank on a book and tangible book basis, so if those metrics haven’t changed much, with the drop in the stock book value and tangible book value are now 2x and 2.4x respectively.

With the drop in the stock to $235, JPM’s dividend yield has jumped over 2%.

In 2024, JPM’s ROTCE (return on tangible capital shareholders' equity) was 22% in Q1, 20% in Q2, 19% in Q3 ’24 and 21% in Q4 ’24. Those are stellar metrics for a bank the size of JPM.

I’m expecting JPM’s ROTCE to decline a little bit in 2025, as the various issues that have caused the market to selloff so far this year, wash through the remaining 3 quarters of the year. Morningstar has a targeted zone of 16-17% for JPM’s ROTCE, but the bank has done consistently better than that in years with a decent market tailwind.

Summary/conclusion

Given that the tariff turmoil didn’t really start until after Q2 ’25 started, the lion’s share of the capital markets disruption was not in Q1 ’25, hence JPM should probably have a decent or at the very least “inline” quarter with current estimates.

Q2 ’25 EPS and revenue estimates are looking for $4.56 in EPS on $43.9 bl in revenue, for expected y-o-y growth of +4% on -11% decline in revenue. JPM printed $49.87 billion in revenue in Q2 ’24, (if that actual net revenue print wasn’t fat-fingered with the spreadsheet entry). So Q2 ’25 guidance might be conservative as it is.

There are so many variables after the “tariff tiff” not the least of which is the bond market troubles, the drop in crude oil, etc., which surfaced this past 10 days, so there are a few reasons – the least of which being tough compares that JPM’s 2025 might be tougher than is expected.

You really have to admire Jamie Dimon though: it appears that a conversation he had with President Trump today, might have helped sway the 90-pause on the non-China tariffs.

Jamie has been pretty bearish since early 2022, when he proclaimed the “economic hurricane” was upon us. There were more bad recession calls by Wall Street in 2022, than I’ve ever seen in my time in the business, and that’s going on 30 years. Dimon is still the best CEO in the business, and one of the top 10 CEOs in the US today.

With so many banks expecting a recession since 2022, the benefit to this has been the larger banks have been adding to loan loss reserves probably earlier than needed (at least 2 years early) so if we do get a recession, the banks are better reserved today than if the onset of the recession was swift like in 2020.

Personally, I think JPM will look at guidance hard on Friday morning. Both for JPM, and for the rest of the S&P 500, there is little reason for CEOs to stretch for a home run this year.

JPM was one of clients' largest holdings as of 3/31/25, but I still think the bank has a “middling” year, as it navigates the political and economic waters.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can and does involve the loss of principal even for short periods of time. All EPS and revenue estimates are sourced from LSEG. None of this information may be updated, and if updated may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cryptocurrencies: Bitcoin Sinks To 5-Month Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

This weekly update tracks some of the largest cryptocurrencies by market share: bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD). While both are considered to be high-risk when it comes to investing, the two have foundational differences that investors should know.

We've also included XRP (XRP-USD), as it was one of the largest cryptocurrencies when this series began. According to Wikipedia, a cryptocurrency is "a digital asset designed to work as a medium of exchange that uses cryptography to secure its transactions, to control the creation of additional units, and to verify the transfer of assets."

Bitcoin

Bitcoin was the world's first cryptocurrency and decentralized digital currency. The first bitcoin transaction occurred in early 2009 and has since grown worldwide to a mainstream financial asset. It is often considered volatile, as seen in our first chart, but one can argue that it is also resilient.

Bitcoin's closing price sunk to its lowest level in five months, dropping over 10% from last week. BTC is down ~19% year to date and ~28% below its record high from January 2025.

Ether

Ether is a cryptocurrency run on the Ethereum blockchain platform and was launched in July 2015. It has the second largest market share, despite being the newest of the three discussed in this article.

Ether's closing price plummeted to a two-year low, falling nearly 23% from last week. ETH is down ~56% year to date and is ~69% below its record high from November 2021.

XRP

XRP, which is owned by Ripple, was launched in 2012 and was one of the larger cryptocurrencies for some time until new coins joined the market.

XRP surged during the last few months of 2024 and the during the first month of 2025, however, it has since pulled back to levels last seen in November. XRP's is down ~23% year to date.

Bitcoin vs. Ether vs. XRP

An index has been created in order to chart these three cryptocurrencies together, considering their significantly different pricing histories. The chart tells us which cryptocurrency's price has changed the most since November 9, 2017. At one point or another, all three have been at the top. At the time of writing, bitcoin is in the lead.

On January 10th, 2024, the SEC approved spot bitcoin ETFs from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB), Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB), Coinshares Valkyrie Bitcoin Fund (BRRR). Here are some of the quick takeaways from the spot bitcoin ETF launch.

On July 23rd, 2024, a handful of spot ether ETFs launched from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Ethereum Trust (ETHE), Franklin Ethereum ETF (EZET), Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW), iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA), Fidelity Ethereum ETF (FETH).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Even Amid Trade War, Nintendo Set To Game On With Switch 2 And Strong IPs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Nintendo Switch 2 will drop on June 5th, and given the strength of the company's intellectual properties (IPs), Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY) (OTCPK:NTDOF) should prove to be a winner, especially in the long run. Further, while the Switch 2 and its accompanying software will surely remain the centerpiece of the company's ambitions, emerging opportunities, including movies starring Nintendo's characters and licensing for theme parks, should bolster Nintendo's revenues. Ultimately, I believe Nintendo offers an attractive opportunity for investors, especially over the next 3 to 4 years as the Switch 2 lifecycle reaches its peak.

The unfolding trade war has already had a major impact on Nintendo, sending prices tumbling in recent days. While the risks of a recession and economic turmoil can't be ignored, the recent decline offers investors an opportunity to pick up Nintendo shares at a discount. A healthy balance sheet and plenty of cash will help Nintendo weather storms if needed, and with digital software sales, which as of now won't be subject to tariffs, being a major source of profits, Nintendo can chart a successful path to profitability even if the trade war continues.

The First Switch Brought Nintendo Back From the Brink

Before the first generation Switch hit the market over 8 years ago, some analysts and industry experts argued that Nintendo's days as a console maker could be numbered and that the company should shift into game development full-time. Then the Nintendo Switch landed, and despite some early skepticism, the hybrid console took off.

Since its release in March 2017, the Nintendo Switch has sold over 150 million units. As far as lifetime sales are concerned, the Switch trails only the PlayStation 2 and Nintendo’s own DS handheld console. Will the Switch 2 match the first generation's success? The future is uncertain, but I'd be surprised if the Switch 2 doesn't turn out to be quite successful in its own right.

With the first Switch, Nintendo managed to move 1.359 billion games. The next-generation Switch will surely revitalize sales, although there's no guarantee that Nintendo will meet high expectations. Still, the large Switch user base, coupled with the strength of Nintendo’s first-party IPs, provides plenty of reasons to be optimistic.

Nintendo's Approach to Development Bodes Well For the Bottom Line

Some developers have struggled with AAA games and their often massive budgets. While Nintendo invests a lot in game development, they've generally avoided keeping up with the Joneses with things like hardware specs and graphics, choosing instead to focus on gameplay. Nintendo also strives to minimize development time and emphasizes efficient development. This should help control costs and protect profit margins.

Another factor to watch is rising game prices. $60 games have been the standard for AAA games for many years now. However, Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom launched at $70 in 2023, and despite the higher price tag, the game still sold 21.55 (and counting) million units.

With the Switch 2, game prices are set to rise further. Donkey Kong Bananza will retail for $70, while Mario Kart World and Kirby and the Forgotten Land have been marked at $80. Let's (conservatively) assume that Nintendo will only mark up the first-party games it expects to be mega-blockbusters. During the first generation, Nintendo had nine games sell more than twenty million copies each. Combined, the top ten best-selling Nintendo games sold over 300 million copies. If Nintendo can achieve similar results (300 million sold) with the Switch 2 and a quarter of these top-selling games are sold at $70 and another quarter at $80 (rather than $60), it’d generate more than $2.2 billion in extra revenue.

If all or most of Nintendo's games end up costing between $70 and $80 and sales numbers match generation 1 sales numbers, Nintendo stands to gain even more. Admittedly, if Nintendo pushes too far, it may face consumer backlash, declining demand, and more people opting to buy second-hand. That said, when you consider that most Nintendo video games provide 10s of hours of entertainment, even an $80 game is relatively affordable entertainment (on a per-hour basis).

Outside of the first-party lineup, for many years, Nintendo struggled to attract external developers to their consoles. Smaller user bases, more limited hardware, and various other factors encouraged external developers to partner with Sony and/or Microsoft instead. Yet, the first Switch garnered strong third-party support, and the new, more robust hardware offered by the Switch 2 should also draw in strong third-party support. Nintendo earns a slice of the third-party sales, including 30% for games sold through its e-shop.

Key Financial Considerations

Nintendo is in solid financial shape, although some of the numbers may, especially at first glance, look rather dour. As shown below, in the first 3 quarters of FY2024, Nintendo’s net sales dropped by 31.4% (YOY) and its operating profit plummeted by 46.7% (YOY).

Console sales dropped 30.6% FY25/Q1-Q3 from a year prior, while software sales declined by 24.4%. However, with the first-generation Switch having already been on the market for so long and with a new console coming in, these declines are not surprising, nor do I find them concerning.

The mounting turmoil in markets, potential financial constraints, and other risks could be a grave threat for many companies in the months ahead. As of its last report, Nintendo had roughly $9.2 billion USD in cash and equivalents, $4.5 billion in short-term investments, and a total of $17.57 billion in current assets, versus $3.95 billion in total current liabilities on its balance sheet.

Meanwhile, Nintendo's total operating expenses (TTM) weigh in at 429,494,000 yen, or just over $2.9 billion USD. With so much cash on hand, Nintendo should be able to weather storms if needed and shouldn't struggle to keep the lights on. High total current assets should provide some cushion if things head south.

Admittedly, Nintendo's TTM P/E GAAP leaves a bit to desire at ~38 compared to ~18 for the industry median. This might seem concerning at first glance, but remember that sales of the 1st gen Switch and software have been tapering off. Nintendo's annual EPS declined from a peak of $.95 in 2021 to $.73 in 2024. The P/E should improve once Switch 2 hits the market and sales/revenues rebound. Recent share price declines also make Nintendo more attractive.

Console Lifecycle Impact on Shares

Video game consoles go through life cycles. Early adopters first pick up consoles when game selection is limited. From there, if the console gains traction, the user base steadily expands. Sometimes, growth is spurred by slashing prices (on both hardware and software), but Nintendo largely resisted price drops with the first-generation Switch.

Crucially, more games entering the market will provide consumers with more options and satisfy more diversified tastes, thus driving software sales. Even for console makers (rather than companies that focus solely on game development), software is a crucial source of revenue and typically offers higher margins than the consoles themselves.

You can see Nintendo's revenues below. The large upward peak observed between 2005 and 2010 was due to the peaking Wii console lifecycle. Later in console lifecycles, revenues may cool, especially as hardware sales decline. In 2024, Nintendo also saw software sales drop 24.4% (per the Financial Results Explanatory Material 3rd Quarter of Fiscal Year Ending March 2025 posted above).

[object HTMLElement]

[object HTMLElement]

Nintendo's net income, as shown above, did decline after peaking during the COVID-19 pandemic. This should come as no surprise, and if anything, net income remained quite strong for an aging console. Later in a console's lifespan, producing hardware and even software should become cheaper, allowing Nintendo to produce more profits off of shrinking revenue. This is observed when looking at Nintendo's annual profit margin.

[object HTMLElement]

Below, you’ll notice four sharp upward swings in Nintendo’s stock price. The first, occurring throughout 2007 and into 2008, coincided with the release and smash success of the Wii gaming system. Nintendo’s stocks slid in 2008, with the Great Recession likely contributing, and then bottomed after Nintendo launched the ill-fated Wii U in late 2012. NTDOY went on an upswing starting in 2017, when the Nintendo Switch was launched and enjoyed considerable success.

The stock spiked again in 2020 and 2021 amid the COVID-19 pandemic, which resulted in increased console adoption and shortages. Following the Switch 2 announcement, Nintendo once again enjoyed an upswing, although stocks have since cooled amid global trade tensions.

The past cannot guarantee the future, but I expect Nintendo’s upswing to generally continue through the rest of the year and into 2026, assuming the global economy avoids falling into a recession. However, the road may be bumpy, especially if trade tensions continue to pace or escalate. Also, Nintendo did experience dips during the first generation Switch lifecycle. Among other things, the initial and somewhat surprising success of the Switch may have led to a bit of exuberance in the markets.

Just looking at share prices in the past and the release of new console generations, one might conclude that even if Nintendo’s share prices rise over the next few years, they will inevitably cool as the Switch 2 reaches the middle of its lifespan. This is a possibility. However, Nintendo’s success has rarely been sustained from one console generation to the next and the company made several missteps over the years, resulting in a sort of boom-and-bust cycle. Going forward, I believe that Nintendo can avoid this boom-and-bust cycle by sticking with what they know works, chiefly including the Nintendo Switch's hybrid home/portable nature.

Nintendo Branching Into Other Forms of Entertainment

Nintendo’s cast of characters includes plenty of big names, including Mario, Zelda, Link, Donkey Kong, and Yoshi. Nintendo is leveraging these assets outside of video games, and with the right moves, the company may be able to establish healthy revenue streams from non-gaming industries.

The Super Mario Bros. movie (2023) pulled in $1.36 billion at the box office on a production budget of just $100 million. A live-action Zelda movie trilogy is rumored to be in the works, with Nintendo collaborating with Sony to bring it to the screen. Still, movies frequently flop and Nintendo may eventually suffer some silver screen stumbles.

While producing successful movies is no easy task, external partners can bring in the necessary talent and cover part of the funding. Universal footed half the bill of the 2023 Mario movie, for example, splitting risks.

The Trade War Will Complicate Things

Let’s acknowledge the elephant in the factory: the ongoing global trade tensions will impact many companies. With Japan currently facing 24% tariffs and Vietnam facing 46% tariffs (Nintendo previously shifted some Switch production from China to Vietnam owing to China-US trade tensions), Nintendo may be forced to mark up the Switch in the United States or eat the loss.

While Microsoft and Sony have often sold their consoles at a loss, Nintendo tries to turn at least some profit on hardware. Yet, even if Nintendo is forced to sell the Switch 2 hardware at a loss, it will still have a viable path to profitability. It’s crucial to note that much of Nintendo’s profits come from games, not the consoles themselves.

Further, physical games may be impacted by tariffs, but for now, games sold through Nintendo's e-shop won't be. With the e-shop, Nintendo won't need to split revenue with brick-and-mortar stores, which typically get 30% of the sale.

Other Risks Investors Should Consider

The Switch 2 could fall short of expectations, and Nintendo’s fate is closely tied to its consoles. Even a minor stumble could snowball. Of course, if a recession hits, Nintendo’s stock will likely be depressed. Video games might be one of the first things families and individuals on tight budgets cut.

The gaming industry has faced headwinds in recent months, with many developers cutting staff. Nintendo has seen sales and revenues cool as well, but this primarily seems to be due to the upcoming release of the Switch 2.

Reverse DCF Model

Based on current prices, free cash flows, a WACC set at 10%, an 8-year growth stage (equal to an 8-year lifecycle for the Switch 2, in line with the Switch 1), and an 8-year terminal stage set at 3.5%, in line with recent global GDP growth, Nintendo will need to grow at 16.32% according to the reverse DCF model. The terms here are rather conservative, with many analysts putting Nintendo's WACC at 6-8%, and the 3.5% terminal stage likely underestimating performance. Simply dropping the WACC to 7% lowers the growth rate to 12.12%.

Nintendo's historical free cash flow growth varied substantially owing to the console lifecycle and COVID-19. The company's free cash flow was reported at $5.7 billion on 03/31/21 before dropping to $2.5 billion on 03/31/22, for example. Still, over the Switch 2's console lifespan, 16.32% should be relatively easy to obtain, and Nintendo can and, I believe, will exceed this performance, which should lead to strong stock performance.

Takeaway: Nintendo Strikes Me as a Long-Term Winner

Especially in the long run, Nintendo strikes me as a solid stock to buy and hold. The upcoming Switch 2 should enjoy plenty of success. That said, unless the Switch 2 exceeds expectations, gains this year could be subdued as the market may have already priced in expectations. Regardless, I expect to see Nintendo's stock rise until at least the midpoint of the Switch 2's lifecycle (about 3–4 years).

Currently, Seeking Alpha’s Wall Street rating summary has one rating for NTDOY with a target price set at $25.40, which is optimistic but not unrealistic. I expect Nintendo's stock to trend upward through the rest of the year, reaching at least the low $20s, and in 2026, sustained momentum should push shares past $25. I would not be surprised if my views turned out to be conservative.

Every investment carries risks, and that is certainly true for Nintendo. The Switch 2 might flop. Or, the trade war could deepen, and Nintendo may find itself in a hard-to-sustain position. Still, Nintendo strikes me as a relatively safe investment, but also one with great upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPLG: Better Than SPY And VOO
data.description:
Trade tariffs and recession fears have put stocks on sale, and the SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500® ETF (NYSEARCA:SPLG) is a fund I have purchased for broad exposure. This is a low-cost alternative to the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY), and even cheaper to own than the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). This article explores why it may be a good time to buy some SPLG for the long term.

The Best of Breed

SPLG was launched in 2005 as a lower-cost alternative to SPY. Both SPLG and SPY are managed by State Street and are nearly identical. It's a similar situation to Invesco's Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) and its lower-cost cousin Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM).

The expense ratio is only 0.02% compared to 0.09% in SPY and 0.03% in VOO. All 3 funds track the S&P 500 Index and have almost identical performance. Perhaps too much is made of the tiny differences in expense ratio. A 0.01% saving over VOO is hardly worth worrying over when they both just made a 10% roundtrip in one session. That said, the longer-term effects can be notable.

Quite simply, I'd rather have the extra 2.3% provided by SPLG. There are some considerations, though. SPY has an AUM of over $500B, while SPLG has a $52B AUM. Liquidity is not an issue with Average Daily Dollar Volume of $700M, but the spread of SPLG is slightly wider with a 30-day median bid/ask spread of 0.02% compared to SPY 0.00%. There are also slight discrepancies in price moves. Again, these are tiny but noticeable over even a 5-day period.

Some of this relates to slight fluctuations in premium/discount to NAV, and there are tiny discrepancies in the weightings of holdings. Can you spot the difference?

Meta Platforms (META) has a 2.65% weighted in SPY and 2.64% in SPLG. It's not significant, but combined with other small stock differences, it explains the diverging price moves.

The Investment Angle

2025 is a risky time to invest as the trade war is escalating and volatility is high. Defensive sectors are faring much better than others in this environment, with such as consumer staples and utilities holding up well, while technology and consumer discretionary are underperforming.

That is bad news for SPLG, as technology is the heaviest-weighted sector by far, and consumer discretionary is the fourth heaviest weighting.

There is some merit in looking for relative strength and avoiding poorly performing parts of the market, but SPLG has now dropped over 20% from its 2025 high and is officially in a bear market. The time to get defensive was several months ago. Buying consumer staples now instead of technology could lead to significant underperformance during any recovery. The recovery after Covid is a good example. Once the panic was over, staples stagnated while technology surged.

The big question is whether the trade war will lead to further pain for stocks, or whether the current fear is overblown. I won't try and sugarcoat the situation: the risks to the U.S. economy and to stock markets are significant. Consumer spending is likely to come under pressure, earnings will slide with higher costs and lower demand, and unemployment could rise. There could be a recession by the end of the year.

There is an alternative. President Trump could walk back some of the proposed tariffs and de-escalate the situation with China. It's not too late to save the economy. Indeed, the U.S. is currently in pretty good shape, with unemployment of just 4.2% and a steady 2% GDP growth. Furthermore, the Fed is gradually cutting rates and corporate earnings are on course for a good year.

Perhaps Trump is maintaining a tough stance to assist with negotiations, or perhaps he really does want to reset global trade and markets. Whatever his motives, the U.S. financial markets can likely change his mind. Stock falls are one thing, but yields spiking higher is another problem, and the exact opposite of Trump's goal. The 10Y yield (US10Y) has just reversed the entire decline from March and April and rallied from a comfortable level of 3.86% to a problematic 4.51% in less than a week.

There are rumors of China selling U.S. Treasuries in response to the 10% tariff Trump just implemented. However, ING makes the point that it is not just China or foreign entities, but a larger theme of "sell America:"

The impact effect of tariffs was the discount for a recession and more rate cuts, and that combination was deemed a good rationale to buy bonds. It still is, and still could be in months to come. But the more troubling narrative of late is the notion of what we call a 'sell America Inc.' risk. It's tough to get a gauge on this, and by the way it does not have to be selling by foreigners, it can also be from domestics. What we can say with certainty is Treasury prices have been bullied lower in the past few days...

Plunging stocks and rising yields are a toxic combination and means there is nowhere safe to hide for investors. Should the situation continue, it will be very hard for Trump to stick to the current course of action, and some concessions over tariffs could lead to a large recovery.

Limited Downside

There is a lot of fear and uncertainty, but every major bottom is the same. I am prepared to take some risks before there is resolution, and this could be a good spot to make a buy in SPLG as it re-tests the 2022 high.

This is speculative, and it may not yet be the time to make sizable investments. However, I will be comfortable holding a small allocation of SPLG and a chart of all the drawdowns of the last 10 years puts the current drop in context and shows it would be very unusual to drop significantly further and not bounce up again.

Conclusions

SPLG is not as well-known or popular as SPY or VOO, but is superior as it has the lowest expense ratio and best total returns since inception. It is my preferred choice for broad stock exposure, and it is currently trading at an attractive price near the 2022 high. Risks are high, but they always are during large drops, and it would be very unusual for SPLG to drop further and not recover again. I rate it a "buy."

data.title: Coinbase: Tariff Fears Overshadow The Subscription Hidden Jewels (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ:COIN) has seen heightened volatility this year amidst a tariff-led selloff. The company posted a banner year in 2024 as cryptocurrencies, led by Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) drew in more institutional capital. Just as it did in the past cryptocurrency bull market, the company has seen transaction revenues - and therefore operating profits - soar dramatically. While bears may be right in pointing out the cyclicality of these revenues and profits, they may be underestimating the value of the subscription and services business, which has also shown strong growth. Unlike transaction revenues, I expect this business to be more reliable and directionally similar to Apple Inc.’s (AAPL) services revenues. I see a clear case for this business to be worth at least the current market cap by itself. I am upgrading COIN to a buy rating.

COIN Stock Price

I last covered COIN in August 2024 where I gave the stock a neutral rating but acknowledged the long-term secular story of Bitcoin. The stock has declined by around 20% since then.

In addition to the underperformance, the company has posted strong growth over the past year, allowing for substantial multiple compression.

COIN Stock Key Metrics

COIN is a brokerage focused on cryptocurrencies that cater to both retail and institutional investors. The latest quarter saw the company close out the 2024-year on a strong note, with revenues jumping 143% and net income jumping 373%. The net margin stood at an incredible 59%.

Skeptics might be quick to note that the high profit margins are evidence of extreme cyclicality, with the company posting operating losses in 2022 and 2023. But that would be ignoring the strong growth seen in the subscriptions and services segment, which saw revenues grow 70.8% YoY in the quarter to $641.1 million, crushing guidance of between $505 million and $580 million. While COIN does not break out the profit margin of this segment, I am of the view that this is a high-margin revenue stream as COIN’s expenses are primarily fixed-cost.

COIN has seen strong growth in stablecoin revenue in part due to the rise of USDC. I note that Circle Internet Group, Inc. (CRCL), the issuer of USDC, has filed plans to IPO.

The company saw operating expenses grow again in 2024 as it invested in growth. I note that the company significantly cut operating expenses in 2023 and 2024 expenses remain well below 2022 levels. The company’s ability to grow its subscription and services business at a rapid pace in 2023 despite the cuts that year further supports my view that it is a high-margin revenue stream.

COIN ended the quarter with $9.3 billion of cash and investments versus $4.2 billion of debt, representing a solid net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided for the first quarter to see between $685 million and $765 million in subscription and services revenue, representing 93% YoY growth at the midpoint. Management also noted that they had generated “approximately $750 million of transaction revenue” through February 11th, roughly halfway through the quarter. Management obviously cautions against extrapolating this data point, but this would imply a quarter similar to the most recent showing.

Is COIN Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

COIN has sold off amidst tariff fears and did see a sizable recovery following the 90-day pause on most of those tariffs. COIN does not have direct exposure to tariffs given its asset-light business model, but cryptocurrency trading has tended to fluctuate wildly based on market conditions. We can see below that crypto market capitalizations are at all-time highs - any potential market downturn might send these values down similarly as they did in 2022.

As of recent prices, COIN traded just around 21x earnings.

Consensus estimates call for around 20% top-line growth this year before flat-lining thereafter.

It is very difficult to value COIN given the high volatility of the transaction revenue business. In this case, I view a sum-of-the-parts valuation to be most appropriate. I project the company to generate at least $3.2 billion in subscription and services revenue in 2025. That would imply just 40% YoY growth and essentially no sequential growth from first quarter guidance. Recently trading at a $44 billion market cap, the entire equity value is trading at just around 14x subscription and services revenue. I view 14x sales as being a reasonable if not conservative multiple given the aggressive growth rates. I value the transaction-fee business at a conservative 5x sales. Based on a 60% net margin assumption, that equates to an 8x earnings multiple. That would lead to roughly $25 billion in value for the transaction revenue business, implying roughly 57% potential upside from current levels to a target price of around $277 per share.

COIN Stock Risks

COIN faces great regulatory and security risks. Governments might crack down on cryptocurrency trading. It is possible that COIN suffered a security breach like many other cryptocurrency platforms over the past several years. It is possible that the subscription and services revenue business does not prove to be as resilient as expected in which case it would not be deserving of a premium multiple. COIN is likely to trade with great volatility on any market or cryptocurrency downturn until its subscription and services revenue business can grow even larger. I note that based on the current run rate, the subscription and services revenue business is not by itself enough to fully cover operating expenses.

COIN Stock Conclusion

The rising legitimacy of cryptocurrencies has placed COIN in the spotlight. The company has positioned itself as a beneficiary from the flow of institutional capital into the space as well as the rise of stablecoins. I see the current market cap as being fully supported by just the subscription and services revenue business. I see considerable potential upside based on what I view to be conservative assumptions. The company's strong secular growth story, deep undervaluation, and strong balance sheet are all characteristics I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

data.title: Italgas: Steady Income Growth Through Market Volatility
data.description:
Introduction

We heard the world was shrinking and everyone was coming closer. This April, we reversed our approach and began thinking locally. We are looking for companies with strong regional businesses and local suppliers that could shield them from what appears to be a trade war between several geopolitical powers in the world.

A company that doesn't seem to be directly affected by the tariffs the Trump administration recently announced is Italgas S.p.A. (OTCPK:ITGGF), the largest gas distributor in Europe.

With the core of its business happening in Italy and Greece, Italgas also plays a key role in the Mediterranean Sea and the gas hub role that Southern Europe is expected to play as Europe diversifies its gas suppliers from Russia.

As I have shown in my previous articles, Italgas operates in a strategic sector such as gas distribution, but has also expanded into the water sector and energy efficiency. Moreover, it is investing in the digitalization of its energy system. Finally, it is a leader in renewable gas solutions development (biomethane and hydrogen). Its "Power-to-Gas" project in Sardinia, for example, uses electricity from renewables to produce hydrogen through the process of electrolysis. The hydrogen that is then produced can be converted into methane or even stored for future usage.

Italgas' FY24 Review

Now, Italgas has been able to report a streak of 30+ consecutive quarters of growth. This proves the resilience and the low volatility of its operations. In particular, the company has been able to increase its margins, and in FY it reported its first-ever year with over EUR 1B in FCF.

Italgas closed its FY2024 with revenues close to EUR 1.8B, with a 14% YoY EBITDA increase to EUR 1.4B and a 20.5% surge in its reported EBIT of EUR 821M.

Its balance sheet is quite solid. The company spends on capex around EUR 900M per year, showcasing ongoing digitization and network enhancement efforts. The net financial debt stood at EUR 6.8B, more or less flat YoY. The average cost of debt increased by 30bps YoY to 1.6%. Yes, it is not a typo, and it shows how convenient it can be to invest in Italgas.

Italgas' Dividend

The strong free cash flow generation enabled Italgas to further improve its dividend policy. As of December 31, 2023, Italgas committed to distributing dividends with a guaranteed minimum 4% growth vs. 2022. Well, now the minimum hike is 5% for 2024 vs. 2023. But the dividend policy also aims at a 65% payout. Italgas will pay the higher of the two possibilities, that is, a 5% hike is guaranteed, but it could also be higher. For example, this upcoming May, Italgas will pay a DPS of EUR 0.406, which is a 15.3% YoY growth.

This dividend policy currently makes the stock yield over 6% (keep in mind the Italian withholding tax is 26%), and offers steady income. This is why Italgas is not selling off big this month, although the recent weakness in the markets is bringing the stock down, making it more alluring.

We should also be aware that the company is moving ahead with a strategic plan through 2030 that should double its top line. At the same time, the expected EBITDA should reach EUR 2.8B, which is a bit over 2x Italgas' 2024 EBITDA. With this expansion of its revenues and profits, the company's leverage should stay between 62% and 65%, with its net debt growing in the teens. This shows that Italgas manages its balance sheet with caution, but can also address a brisk growth pace.

Therefore, I think we can reasonably expect the dividend to grow at a faster pace than the minimum threshold (a 5% growth through 2030 would give a DPS of EUR 0.544, an 8.5% yield based on today's stock price in Milan hovering around EUR 6.40). I think we would not be too bold to expect the company to report at least EUR 1B in net income in 2030. Assuming the share count stays the same at 811M, we could have a 2030 DPS of EUR 1.20. Let's say this is too optimistic, we could expect the DPS to be around EUR 0.87, which is the average of the two hypotheses we discussed. The fwd yield would be 13.6%. Not bad at all, especially considering there is no sign of Italgas being a value trap.

Valuation

Currently, Italgas trades at a P/E ratio of around 10 and has a market cap of EUR 5.4B. Sometimes, the stock dips to below EUR 5.00 per share, but recently, Italgas has attracted new investors thanks to its reliable dividend policy. In April, it gave up only 6% of its stock price, which was heading north toward EUR 7.00. Assuming FY25 net profits of EUR 620M (around a 15% YoY increase), we would have Italgas trading at a fwd PE just below 9x. I think this valuation is reasonable, especially for a defensive stock that is poised to keep generating income through market volatility. Moreover, its network effects linked to its leading position in Southern Europe should help it capitalize even further on the trend that sees Europe looking at Africa and the Middle East for its gas supplies. As a result, I continue to rate Italgas as a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AbbVie: Thriving Beyond Humira's Patent Cliff (Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

AbbVie Inc. (NYSE:ABBV) stock is up 16% compared to S&P 500 returns of 9.41% since my last look in November 2023. I evaluated AbbVie after its $10 billion acquisition of cancer developer, ImmunoGen. ImmunoGen’s flagship drug, Elahere, was a first-in-class antibody-drug conjugate, or ADC, approved for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Its pipeline included other ADCs. The acquisition came when AbbVie was facing intense competition for its blockbuster drug, Humira, after its loss of exclusivity (LOE). The giant pharmaceutical company has kept its pockets open. Just a month after acquiring ImmunoGen, AbbVie splurged on Cerevel Therapeutics and its neuroscience-focused pipeline (including emraclidine and tavapadon for Alzheimer’s disease psychosis and Parkinson’s disease, respectively). The $8.7 billion deal was completed last August.

While AbbVie remains engaged in legal battles concerning Humira biosimilars, Humira revenues continue to decline. At its peak, Humira was generating $21 billion (2022). So, it was easily one of the best-selling drugs in all history. Biosimilars entered the market in 2023, causing price and market share erosion. In 2024, Humira product revenues dropped ~38% to reach just $8.993 billion. However, judging by its stock performance and growth in other key products, AbbVie has done a solid job making up for Humira’s LOE.

Rising Immunology Stars: Skyrizi and Rinvoq

Other immunology drugs, like Skyrizi and Rinvoq, have blossomed into monster drugs. Their growth has completely made up for Humira’s loss. Skyrizi (plaque psoriasis, Crohn’s, and ulcerative colitis) revenues jumped 50.9% year-over-year to reach $11.718 billion in 2024. Likewise, Rinvoq (arthritis, atopic dermatitis) grew 50.5% year-over-year to reach $5.971 billion.

AbbVie is expanding Rinvoq’s label to include Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA). Just yesterday, the company announced that the European Commission approved Rinvoq, making it the only JAK inhibitor approved in the E.U. Current GCA treatment recommendations call for steroids, so Rinvoq's approval could alter the treatment landscape. Relative to other immunology indications, GCA is a much smaller market opportunity, coming in at under $1 billion.

Impressively, AbbVie projects that Rinvoq and Skyrizi, together, can achieve over $31 billion in revenue in 2027. This represents a CAGR of approximately 21% and a raise of $4 billion from its last projection.

Oncology Portfolio Evolution

AbbVie’s oncology segment features Imbruvica, a first-generation Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor used predominantly for liquid tumors. This drug is now facing competition from second-generation BTK inhibitors like AstraZeneca PLC's (AZN) Calquence (which saw 25% year-over-year growth in 2024). So, although the overall market for BTK inhibitors is large and growing, AbbVie’s Imbruvica is slowly losing market share. Indeed, $3.347 billion in revenues for Imbruvica represented a 6.9% year-over-over decline in 2024.

Thankfully, steady growth from Venclexta (up 12.9% to reach $2.583 billion) and a robust launch from Elahere have made up for Imbruvica. The latter achieved $477 million in 2024, which was a partial year. Together, AbbVie’s oncology portfolio grew an impressive 10.8%.

Neuroscience and Aesthetics: Additional Growth Pillars

AbbVie’s neuroscience efforts are paying off and seeing robust growth (up 16.6% year-over-year) thanks particularly to Botox Therapeutic (migraines, overactive bladder, spasticity) and Vraylar (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder adjunct).

AbbVie’s aesthetics portfolio, which features Botox Cosmetic and Juvederm, held steady (-2.2% year-over-year) despite fierce biosimilar competition in this market. Moreover, this segment is probably more vulnerable than others to macroeconomic headwinds. People spend less on aesthetics during difficult economic times. Despite this, AbbVie still envisions a “high single-digit CAGR for aesthetics through 2029.”

Earnings

Taking a closer look at Q4 earnings, revenues totaled $15.1 billion (up 6.1% year-over-year). Due to a $3.5 billion after-tax partial impairment charge related to emraclidine after it failed two Phase 2 schizophrenia trials, AbbVie’s GAAP diluted loss per share was $0.02. This specifically highlights the difficulty in neuroscience drug development and the inherent risks associated with acquiring clinical-stage biotechnology companies.

Earlier this week, AbbVie adjusted its guidance for full year 2025 and Q1 2025 as follows:

Financial Health

As of December 31, AbbVie held $5.52 billion in cash and equivalents. Total current assets were $25.58 billion, while total current liabilities were $38.75 billion. Subsequently, AbbVie’s current ratio is under 1.0, which is not ideal. Long-term debt and finance lease obligations totaled $60.34 billion.

AbbVie consistently generates between $18 billion and $22 billion in cash from operations each year.

Due to robust cash generation, it can afford some wiggle room when it comes to short-term liquidity and leverage.

Valuation Assumptions

Upon discounted cash flow analysis, there is a slight disconnect between what growth expectations are implied by AbbVie’s intrinsic value and what analysts project.

Assuming just 3% revenue growth (on average over my nine-year projection period) and free cash flow (FCF) margins of 31% (where it has been historically), AbbVie appears undervalued by ~10%.

My sensitivity analysis reveals there is a good amount of margin of safety in the event key assumptions (i.e., growth and margin) change.

Additionally, there is an asymmetric upside (2.20x) if AbbVie exceeds baseline expectations (i.e., 3% revenue growth).

My Monte Carlo simulation accounts for variability among key inputs like growth, margins, beta, and cost of debt. So, it provides a more realistic and dynamic view of possible share price outcomes.

The upside-to-downside ratio (% change from current price to 95th and 5th percentile share prices) of 3.1:1 implies a favorable risk/reward profile. This also tells us that while AbbVie may be undervalued, its valuation is within reason. Overall, there is a better chance for an upside than there is a downside.

Conclusion

Despite some hiccups (e.g., Phase 2 outcomes for the Cerevel assets, near-term EPS pressure related to acquired IPR&D, and asset impairments from M&A activity), AbbVie continues to see robust growth in key segments. For many companies, Humira’s LOE would have been difficult to offset. Through consistent innovation and leadership in immunology (e.g., Skyrizi and Rinvoq) and strategic expansion into oncology and neuroscience, AbbVie has navigated Humira’s LOE gracefully.

There are some risks to consider. AbbVie’s aggressive M&A has resulted in a substantial debt burden, which could reduce financial flexibility. Humira’s revenue erosion could accelerate faster than expected. Its aesthetics portfolio, which declined in 2024, could be vulnerable to macroeconomic pressures on discretionary spending or increasing competition.

Overall, AbbVie's outlook leans positive. The stock appears a bit undervalued per DCF modeling and also offers an attractive 3.73% dividend yield. Its robust cash generation enables the advancement of key pipeline assets, creating a flywheel effect that cannot easily be replicated (as seen with drugs like Skyrizi replacing the revenue losses from Humira). AbbVie is a Buy and can be a solid addition to any diversified portfolio.

data.title: RBC Bearings: A True Quality Stock At A Demanding Valuation
data.description:
In the summer of 2023, I believed that RBC Bearings Incorporated (NYSE:RBC) was rolling on. The producer of high-quality niche products was moving along just fine, and fast forwarding to today the business has seen solid (earnings) growth, being awarded more lofty valuations with the passage of time.

This means that expectations have risen quite a bit and while RBC stock undoubtedly is of great quality, I fear that current prices reflect a bit too much of that quality here.

A Specialized Product Business

RBC Bearings is a producer of highly engineered precision bearings, components, and essential systems which are used in industrial, aerospace and defense industries. The business has only been around 34 years, and has been very successfully led by CEO Michael Hartnett.

The company has been publicly listed since 2005, as it has seen impressive growth thanks to an impressive acquisition streak which totals nearly 30 transactions.

As of today, the company generates about $1.6 billion in sales and is largely a domestic business, with nearly 90% of sales generated in the U.S. About two-thirds of sales are generated from industrial applications, complemented by the aerospace & defense business. The business is split 50/50 even between OEM and distribution/aftermarket services.

Bearings are key, used in various machines, aircraft and systems as these products allow for motion, reduced wear to moving parts, while reducing damage and energy loss at large. Key OEMs, which use its products, include names like Caterpillar, Boeing, Airbus, and other big related names.

Over the past three decades, the company has grown sales at an impressive CAGR around 11% per annum. All this has translated into remarkable returns for investors, as a $15 stock at the time of the public offering in 2005 has risen to levels in the $300s here.

This comes as investors over time have digested a bit of dilution as well, largely resulting from inorganic growth efforts made by the company, including a huge $2.9 billion deal to acquire Doge. This deal, which was announced in 2021, involved the purchase of the mechanical power transmission unit from ABB after the pandemic.

The deal resulted in high leverage ratios around 4 times, as the acquisition only contributed in full to the 2023 results, with revenues up 55% to $1.47 billion that year, with organic growth reported at 11%. Adjusted earnings were reported at $218 million, equal to $7.48 per share. Net debt was down to $1.3 billion already, for a 3.0 times leverage ratio.

With the business having digested the deal well, the company was on track to generate about $1.6 billion in sales, with earnings seen up around $8 per share. Trading at $217 in August 2023 shares were not cheap at 24-27 times earnings, assuming that earnings would come in between $8-$9 per share, while there still was some leverage apparent on the balance sheet.

Moving Steady

Since the summer of 2023, shares have gradually made further inroads as they rose to absolute highs of a $370 in the fall of last year, now having fallen back to the $305 mark, amidst the turmoil caused by the new tariffs and perhaps full-blown trade war.

Early in 2024, the company posted its fiscal 2024 results, a year in which revenues were up 6% to $1.56 billion. Adjusted earnings were up about a dollar to $8.62 per share. About $17 million of the reconciliation, or about half a dollar after-tax, was adjusted in relation to stock-based compensation expenses, which made realistic earnings trended around $8 per share.

Net debt was down to $1.13 billion and with adjusted EBITDA improving to $482 million, it was the combination of absolute net debt being reduced and improved earnings, which reduced leverage ratios all the way to 2.3 times. The company guided for a 7-8% increase in first quarter sales for the fiscal year 2025, but failed to provide a full-year guidance.

What Now?

The results released so far for the fiscal 2025 were not too inspiring, with revenues up 4% and change through the first nine months to $1.20 billion. This was due to industrial sales which came in flattish, in fact, were down slightly on the year, with aerospace & defense sales up in a spectacular fashion.

GAAP earnings rose in a pronounced fashion, aided by the elimination of preferred stock (and its dividends) as adjusted earnings so far this year were up a dollar to $7.16 per share, coming in a long way to exceed $9.50 per share, although that the adjustment for stock-based compensation is a bit on the rise, making me work with a realistic earnings numbers just short of $9 per share.

Net debt is down to $940 million and with this metric coming in at $380 million over the past nine months, I peg leverage ratios at just 1.8 times, after the company has swiftly deleveraged following the deal with ABB, while it has been quiet on the dealmaking front since that larger deal.

Right now, shares trade at around 33 times earnings, if they are realistic at $9 per share, which is not very cheap even as the business shows 5% organic growth and leverage has come down a great deal. This comes as the earnings yield has compressed, amidst the sound performance and deleveraging attempts.

Even if shares are down 20% from the highs, shares are far from cheap here, also recognizing that shares only broke through the $300 mark in November of last year. While it can be argued that the company has a great aerospace and defense business, the counterargument is that industrial sales come in flattish, making a sum-of-the-part analysis likely not valid as well.

A Final Thought

Right now, I am erring cautious, given the relative outperformance of the business. I generally like the business, not just based on the performance in recent years, but really the fundamentals of the business and track record over the past decades.

While RBC Bearings Incorporated is arguably of great quality, the issue is that the business is awarded lofty valuations here at just a 3% earnings yield. This could be justified by the strong performance and leverage having come down.

Weighing it all together, a great business has been awarded rising valuation multiples over time, which makes me cautious here. I look forward to updating the thesis upon a period of stagnation or share pullbacks here.
